lines.greek: λυγρῆς, ἥ τέ μοι αἰεὶ ἐνὶ στήθεσσι φίλον κῆρ
τείρει, ἐπεί με μάλιστα καθίκετο πένθος ἄλαστον.
τοίην γὰρ κεφαλὴν ποθέω μεμνημένη αἰεί,
ἀνδρός, τοῦ κλέος εὐρὺ καθʼ Ἑλλάδα καὶ μέσον Ἄργος.
τὴν δʼ αὖ Τηλέμαχος πεπνυμένος ἀντίον ηὔδα·
μῆτερ ἐμή, τί τʼ ἄρα φθονέεις ἐρίηρον ἀοιδὸν
τέρπειν ὅππῃ οἱ νόος ὄρνυται; οὔ νύ τʼ ἀοιδοὶ
αἴτιοι, ἀλλά ποθι Ζεὺς αἴτιος, ὅς τε δίδωσιν
ἀνδράσιν ἀλφηστῇσιν, ὅπως ἐθέλῃσιν, ἑκάστῳ.
τούτῳ δʼ οὐ νέμεσις Δαναῶν κακὸν οἶτον ἀείδειν·
τὴν γὰρ ἀοιδὴν μᾶλλον ἐπικλείουσʼ ἄνθρωποι,
ἥ τις ἀκουόντεσσι νεωτάτη ἀμφιπέληται.
σοὶ δʼ ἐπιτολμάτω κραδίη καὶ θυμὸς ἀκούειν·
οὐ γὰρ Ὀδυσσεὺς οἶος ἀπώλεσε νόστιμον ἦμαρ
ἐν Τροίῃ, πολλοὶ δὲ καὶ ἄλλοι φῶτες ὄλοντο.
ἀλλʼ εἰς οἶκον ἰοῦσα τὰ σʼ αὐτῆς ἔργα κόμιζε,
ἱστόν τʼ ἠλακάτην τε, καὶ ἀμφιπόλοισι κέλευε
ἔργον ἐποίχεσθαι· μῦθος δʼ ἄνδρεσσι μελήσει
πᾶσι, μάλιστα δʼ ἐμοί· τοῦ γὰρ κράτος ἔστʼ ἐνὶ οἴκῳ.
ἡ μὲν θαμβήσασα πάλιν οἶκόνδε βεβήκει·

interlinear: the-woeful, which for-me always in my-breast the-dear heart
wears-away, since me most-of-all has-come-upon grief unforgettable.
for-such a-head I-long-for, remembering always,
of-the-man, whose fame is-wide through Hellas and mid-Argos.'
and-her in-turn Telemachus, prudent, answering addressed:
'Mother mine, why then do-you-grudge the-trusty singer
to-delight wherever his mind stirs-him? not indeed singers
are-to-blame, but somehow Zeus is-to-blame, who gives
to-men, bread-eaters, however he-wills, to-each.
and-for-this-man no blame-is-it of-the-Danaans the-evil doom to-sing;
for-that song the-more do-men praise,
whichever to-hearers newest comes-about.
and-for-you let-endure your-heart and spirit to-listen;
for-not Odysseus alone lost his-homecoming day
in Troy, but many also other men perished.
but to-your-house going, your-own tasks attend-to,
the-loom and the-distaff, and-to-your-handmaids give-order
their-work to-ply; and-speech to-men shall-be-a-care,
to-all, and-most-of-all to-me; for-mine is the-power in this-house.'
she then, amazed, back to-her-house had-gone;
literal: this woeful song, which always wears the heart in my breast,
since a grief I cannot forget has fallen upon me above all.
For such a head I long, remembering always,
the man whose fame is wide through Hellas and mid-Argos.'
Then prudent Telemachus answered her:
'My mother, why do you grudge the trusty singer
to give delight wherever his mind stirs him? Singers
are not to blame; Zeus somehow is to blame, who gives
to men who eat bread, to each as he wills.
It is no blame in this man to sing the evil doom of the Danaans;
for men praise that song the most
which comes newest to those who hear it.
Let your heart and spirit endure to listen;
for Odysseus was not the only one who lost his day of homecoming
at Troy, but many other men perished also.
But go to your house and attend to your own tasks,
the loom and the distaff, and bid your handmaids
ply their work; speech shall be the care of men,
of all, and most of all of me, for mine is the power in this house.'
Then she, amazed, went back to her house;
translations.elizabethan:
this woeful song, that wears the heart away
within my breast, for grief past all forgetting
has fallen upon me. Such a head I want,
and keep his memory always: he whose fame
is wide through Hellas and through mid-Argos."
Then prudent Telemachus made answer thus:
"My mother, why begrudge the faithful singer
his pleasure, where his mind may carry him?
It is not singers who are to be blamed;
it is Zeus, in some sort, who gives to men
that eat their bread, to each one as he wills.
No blame is his to sing the Danaans' doom.
Men praise that song the highest which comes newest
upon the ears of those who sit and hear.
Let then thy heart endure to hear it out.
Odysseus was not only man to lose
his homeward day at Troy; there perished many.
But go into thy house, and take in hand
thine own work, loom and distaff, and command
thy women to their labour. Speech shall be
the care of men, of all men, most of me,
for mine is the authority in this house."
Then she, amazed, went back into the house.
translations.modernist:
this miserable song. It wears out the heart in my breast. A grief came on me that I cannot get past. I want that one head back. I remember it all the time. His fame is wide, through Hellas and the middle of Argos."

Then Telemachus answered her. He had sense.

"Mother. Why do you grudge the singer his pleasure, wherever his mind takes him? It is not the singers who are to blame. Zeus is to blame. He gives to men who eat bread, to each one what he wants to give.

There is nothing wrong with singing about the bad end of the Danaans. Men praise the newest song. That is how it works. Let your heart hold up and listen to it.

Odysseus was not the only man who lost his day of coming home at Troy. A great many others died there.

Now go into the house and see to your own work. The loom. The distaff. Tell your women to get on with theirs. Talking is men's business. All men's, and mine most of all. The power in this house is mine."

She was astonished, and she went back into the house.
translations.storybook:
this miserable song. It wears out my poor heart, for a sadness came upon me that I can never forget. There is one dear head I long for, and I remember him all the time, the man whose fame is known far and wide, through Hellas and all the middle of Argos."

Then sensible young Telemachus answered her.

"Mother, why do you grudge the good singer his song, wherever his mind takes him? It is not the singers who are to blame for anything. It is Zeus who is to blame, for he gives to every man who eats bread whatever he chooses to give.

There is nothing wrong with singing about the sad end of the Danaans. People always praise the newest song best. So let your heart be brave and listen to it.

Odysseus was not the only man who never got home from Troy, you know. Ever so many others died there too.

Now go back into the house and see to your own work, your loom and your spinning, and tell your maids to get on with theirs. Talking is for the men to do. For all of them, and most of all for me, because I am the master of this house."

And she was so astonished that she went back into the house.

murray.text: Then wise Telemachus answered her: “My mother, why dost thou begrudge the good minstrel to give pleasure in whatever way his heart is moved? It is not minstrels that are to blame, but Zeus, I ween, is to blame, who gives to men that live by toil, to each one as he will.

With this man no one can be wroth if he sings of the evil doom of the Danaans; for men praise that song the most which comes the newest to their ears. For thyself, let thy heart and soul endure to listen; for not Odysseus alone lost

in Troy the day of his return, but many others likewise perished. Nay, go to thy chamber, and busy thyself with thine own tasks, the loom and the distaff, and bid thy handmaids ply their tasks; but speech shall be for men, for all, but most of all for me; since mine is the authority in the house.”

She then, seized with wonder, went back to her chamber, for she laid to heart the wise saying of her son. Up to her upper chamber she went with her handmaids, and then bewailed Odysseus, her dear husband until flashing-eyed Athena cast sweet sleep upon her eyelids.
